name: Food History Plot Twists Pack
description: Questions about the food you eat and the weird paths it took to get on your plate.
author: Tipsy Trivia

questions.prompt: Chocolate was a drink for most of history. When was the first solid chocolate bar made?
questions.explanation: The first solid chocolate bar was created by British company J.S. Fry & Sons in 1847. People had been drinking chocolate since at least 1500 BCE without thinking 'what if it was a rectangle?'
questions.hook_line: For most of human history the chocolate bar didn't exist, and this fact should cause more outrage than it does.
questions.why_weird: 3,500 years of everyone drinking chocolate and nobody made it solid until the 1840s. We were one idea away the whole time.

questions.prompt: Lobsters were once so plentiful they were considered what in colonial America?
questions.explanation: In colonial America, lobsters washed up on beaches in piles two feet deep. They were fed to prisoners and servants. Massachusetts servants demanded contracts limiting how often they were served lobster.
questions.hook_line: The most expensive restaurant seafood was once considered too embarrassing to serve to guests.
questions.why_weird: Lobster went from prison food to a $60 entrée in about 150 years. The lobster didn't change. Our scarcity did.
questions.source_title: Smithsonian: When Lobster Was Poverty Food

questions.prompt: Caesar salad was invented in which country?
questions.explanation: Caesar salad was invented in 1924 by Caesar Cardini at his restaurant in Tijuana, Mexico. It was improvised from available ingredients on July 4th to feed a large hungry crowd.
questions.hook_line: Every fancy restaurant Caesar salad traces its heritage to a Tijuana improvisation on July 4th.
questions.why_weird: The salad named after a Roman emperor was invented by an Italian in Mexico on American Independence Day. None of those facts belong together.
questions.source_title: LA Magazine: True Origin of Caesar Salad

questions.prompt: Bananas are technically what type of plant?
questions.explanation: Banana plants are the world's largest herbaceous plants — giant grasses. What looks like a trunk is a 'pseudostem' made of tightly packed leaf bases. The plant has no woody tissue at all.
questions.hook_line: The banana does not grow on a tree. Nothing about the banana is what you think it is.
questions.why_weird: You are eating a grass fruit. Everything is different now.

questions.prompt: Pineapples were so rare in 18th-century England that wealthy people did what with them?
options: Rented them to display at parties
questions.explanation: A single pineapple could cost £5,000 equivalent today. Aristocrats rented pineapples by the night to carry them to dinner parties as status symbols — not to eat, just to display. The same pineapple rotated through multiple parties before being sold.
questions.hook_line: Pineapples were once so expensive you could rent one for the evening. Just to carry it.
questions.why_weird: Rich Englishmen rented a fruit, showed it off, and returned it uneaten. It was a pure status flex for tropical produce.
questions.source_title: Historic England: Pineapple as Status Symbol

questions.prompt: John Harvey Kellogg invented cornflakes in the 1890s for what original purpose?
questions.explanation: Kellogg developed cornflakes as a bland food intended to suppress desire as part of his anti-vice health regimen at his sanitarium. He believed plain food would reduce urges. He was wrong, but cornflakes survived.
questions.hook_line: Your breakfast cereal exists because a Victorian doctor was very concerned about impulse control.
questions.why_weird: Cornflakes were invented as a medical intervention and are now international breakfast. The reason is on cereal boxes nowhere.
questions.source_title: Smithsonian: Surprising Origins of Corn Flakes
